does: This paper introduces GLiGuard, a 0.3B-parameter bidirectional encoder model that reformulates LLM content moderation as a schema-conditioned classification task rather than autoregressive generation. The core contribution is encoding task definitions and label semantics as structured token schemas in the input, enabling simultaneous evaluation of multiple safety dimensions—prompt safety, response safety, refusal detection, 14 harm categories, and 11 jailbreak strategies—in a single non-autoregressive forward pass. The model, adapted from GLiNER2, achieves competitive F1 scores across nine established safety benchmarks while dramatically reducing latency and parameter count compared to existing 7B–27B parameter guardrail models that use sequential text generation.
connects: This work addresses a long-standing problem in LLM safety where moderation has been treated as a generation task despite being fundamentally a classification problem. It builds on recent advances in efficient task-aware encoders like GLiNER2, which demonstrated that encoding task specifications directly into input representations enables versatile downstream performance. The paper responds to the limitations of autoregressive guardrail approaches that struggle with latency and scalability, connecting to broader research on efficient safety alignment and the trend toward parameter-efficient safety mechanisms that parallels work on efficient inference and adapter-based tuning in large models.
enables: GLiGuard opens several research directions in scalable LLM safety. Its efficient architecture enables real-time moderation at scale, supporting deployment in resource-constrained settings and continuous monitoring of multi-aspect safety. The schema-conditioned design points toward compositional safety frameworks where new harm categories or safety dimensions can be added at inference time without retraining, potentially enabling dynamic safety policies. The approach also suggests future work on combining classification-based moderation with generative explanations, hierarchical harm taxonomies, and cross-lingual safety evaluation, while establishing a new paradigm for thinking about safety as structured classification rather than open-ended generation.
